focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The porch light made the snow look dirtier than it was. Nora stood in the yellow cone in hospital sweatpants and a down vest, her left wrist braced in black elastic the way it had been all fall, as if the joint had grown its own second skin. She kept flexing the fingers while Eli ratcheted June's last plastic bin into the back of the Forester with the bungie cord Theo had given him. Every hook clicked like a tiny trigger.

June came out with a mesh laundry sack on one shoulder and a paper bag from the kitchen folded tight at the top. She was in that old maroon beanie from high school track, the one with the torn seam she never fixed. Her left eyebrow nick flashed when she looked up at him under the porch light. It made her look skeptical even before she said anything.

"You packed the extension cord, right?" she asked.

"Blue tote, side pocket. Next to the desk lamp." He heard himself answering in inventory language again. "And the kettle's wrapped in that towel with the bad hem."

"Good. The dorm kettle was a biohazard by November."

Nora held out the car key. "Roads are slushy past the river. Salt trucks were late on 376. Don't cut through the tunnel lane if traffic stacks."

She still did that with him sometimes, gave instructions she knew he already understood. It was not trust. It was governance. He took the key, thumb rubbing the split plastic fob, like a man receiving shift notes.

"Call when you get there," Nora said to June.

"I always do."

Nora looked at Eli, paused, then adjusted the brace strap with her teeth. "You have the parking pass envelope. If they tow, nobody has money for heroic mistakes."

"It's in the glove box."

She watched him long enough that he could feel the old version of himself trying to step forward and perform steadiness....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...oodie dropped a fork and swore softly; his mother laughed and handed him a napkin, and the kid pretended he was too old to need help. Eli looked away because the moment was ordinary and unbearable in equal measure.

After lights-out he lay in the lower berth this time and let the ache in his side spread where it wanted. He did not rehearse. He followed the route in his head instead: old steel towns, river bends, the tunnel before Pittsburgh where the windows turned to mirrors and every passenger had to look at themselves for thirty seconds whether they wanted to or not.

Late the next afternoon the train slid along the Monongahela under a low white sky. He saw warehouses, graffitied retaining walls, church spires, and the familiar ragged edges of home. His phone buzzed once.

Theo: You in yet?

Eli: Ten minutes out.

Theo: Remember what I said.

Eli: I do.

His phone buzzed again with Nora's name.

Nora: One condition before you come to the house. Wait outside until I open the door. Put your bag in the basement first. Do not go to June's room tonight. If you break that, you don't stay here.

Eli: Understood. I'll wait outside, take my bag to the basement, and stay away from June's room. If I break it, I'll get a motel.

He zipped the green folder into his duffel, checked for the receipts one more time, then stopped himself from checking again. On the platform at Pittsburgh the cold was wetter than out west, a river cold that climbed through shoes. People surged toward pickups and ride shares, toward hugs, toward nobody.

Eli stood still long enough to let the crowd pass around him. The train door folded shut behind his back with a pneumatic sigh. He adjusted the duffel strap off his sore shoulder and walked toward the station exit without calling anyone to come get him.
